But in the Google version, we only used four input tokens and 11 output tokens.", '. Every LLM has a different token vocabulary. And these tokens are all of the different words, subwords, and characters that it knows. Each of these words and subwords gets assigned a number. And the number is the token. When we call an LLM, it actually encodes the text that we send it into tokens. So, hello world gets split up into its largest individual tokens in the vocabulary. In this case, that might mean hello, space, world, and exclamation mark. And then it identifies the numbers in the vocabulary that match that word or subword.']
response: In the context of a Large Language Model, tokens are the individual units that make up the vocabulary of the model. These tokens can be words, subwords, or even characters that the model is familiar with. Each of these tokens is assigned a unique number, which is used to represent the token when the model processes text. When you input text into a Large Language Model, it breaks down the text into its constituent tokens, and then matches these tokens to their corresponding numbers in the model's vocabulary. For example, the text "hello world" might be split into tokens such as "hello", "space", "world", and possibly even an "exclamation mark" if present, and then these tokens are identified by their corresponding numbers in the vocabulary.
reference: Tokens are the currency of a LLM. the speaker gives an example of text hello word being split into 3 tokens: hello, word, and a space. the more tokens you use, the more expensive it is to run the LLM.

user_input: what is the full llm process?
retrieved_contexts: ["Let's look at a diagram of the full LLM process end to end. We pass in hello world. It gets split into the largest chunks that it knows about in the vocabulary. Those then get encoded individually into tokens by just looking up the number assigned to them in the token vocabulary. Then the LLM does some thinking here and it outputs some more tokens. In other words, all the computation here is being done on numbers, not on text"]
response: The full LLM process involves several steps. It starts by taking in input, such as "hello world", and splitting it into the largest chunks that it knows about in the vocabulary. These chunks then get encoded individually into tokens by looking up the number assigned to them in the token vocabulary. After that, the LLM performs some computation on these tokens, which are essentially numbers, and outputs some more tokens. This process is entirely numerical, meaning that all the computation is being done on numbers, not on the original text.
reference: the input string is first split into largest chunks of text that it knows and then encoded into tokens by looking at the numbers being assigned to them and then the llm does some thinking and gives an output string which is then decoded back into text and returned to the user.

response:
A tokenizer is essentially a component that breaks down input text into smaller units called tokens. These tokens can be words, subwords, or even characters, and each one is assigned a unique number. The tokenizer's job is to identify the largest possible subwords or words in the input text and match them with the corresponding tokens in its vocabulary. 

In the context of Large Language Models, the tokenizer plays a crucial role in encoding the input text into tokens that the model can understand. This process involves splitting the text into its constituent parts, such as words, subwords, and characters, and then assigning a numerical token to each part. The goal of the tokenizer is to efficiently tokenize the input text, allowing the model to process and generate text based on the encoded tokens. 

It's worth noting that tokenizers can be trained on large corpora of text data, which enables them to learn the patterns and structures of language. This training process allows tokenizers to identify common subwords, word combinations, and character sequences, and assign tokens accordingly. As a result, different model providers may have different tokenizers, each with its own unique vocabulary and tokenization approach.
reference: A tokenizer is a component of an LLM that splits text into tokens and assigns them numerical IDs.

user_input: how does a tokenizer behave when it encounters unsual words or phrases?
retrieved_contexts: ["And so, unusual words are split up into more tokens than words that occur more frequently in the data set. This also means that if you're querying the LLM in a language that's not present in the data set much, it's likely to break it up into more tokens because it's not used to those combinations of letters.", "Finally, let's look at how a tokenizer behaves when it encounters an unusual word. This is o fragous day from the Louiswis Carol poem. Frajus is just a word that Lewis Carol made up. So, it's not going to be very frequent in the data set. When we run this through 0200K, Fragus is actually split up into four separate tokens here. And so, unusual words are split up into more tokens than words that occur more frequently in the data set"]
response: When a tokenizer encounters unusual words or phrases, it breaks them up into more tokens compared to words that occur more frequently in the data set. This is because the tokenizer is not familiar with these unusual combinations of letters, having not seen them often in the data set. As a result, it splits them into multiple tokens. For example, the made-up word "Fragus" is split into four separate tokens, illustrating how unusual words are handled by being divided into more tokens than common words. This behavior also applies to languages that are not well-represented in the data set, where the tokenizer may break up words into more tokens due to its lack of familiarity with those languages.
reference: When a tokenizer encounters unusual words or phrases that it has not seen during training, it may struggle to split them into meaningful tokens. In such cases, the tokenizer might break the word into smaller subwords or even individual characters, which can lead to an increase in the number of tokens used and potentially affect the performance of the LLM.
